# Block 2: Achieve Performance {background-image="assets/symbol_python_killers.png" background-opacity="0.3" background-size="cover" background-color="#2d4059"}

Now we know **where** the time goes.
Here's how to make it faster — in Python and in C++.

<!-- Sources for Python Performance Killers:
     - All code examples and benchmark numbers:
       ../deep-research-report-python-performance-tricks.md
     - Benchmark table (CPython 3.13.5, Linux x86_64): same report §"Benchmark table"
     - set membership (1034x): report §"Prefer set/dict for membership checks"
     - string concat (18x): report §"Avoid quadratic string concatenation"
     - sum builtin (3.9x): report §"Prefer built-in reductions"
     - lru_cache (50000x): report §"Use functools.lru_cache"
     - NumPy (5000x): report §"Use NumPy for numeric hotspots"
     - timeit list-vs-set output: re-measured on this machine (1211x)
     - Summary chart: generated with matplotlib (gen_summary_charts.py)
-->
# Python Performance Killers {background-image="assets/symbol_python_killers2.png" background-opacity="0.3" background-size="cover" background-color="#2d4059"}

Common patterns that are silently expensive.
Each one is easy to fix — **once you know it's there**.

## `x in list` is secretly O(n)

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="48%"}
**Slow:**
```python
users = list(range(10_000))
count = 0
for q in queries:
    if q in users:       # linear scan!
        count += 1
```
:::

::: {.column width="4%"}
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
**Fast:**
```python
users = set(range(10_000))
count = 0
for q in queries:
    if q in users:       # hash lookup
        count += 1
```
:::
::::

::: {.fragment}
```text
list: 128.6 ms   set: 0.12 ms   speedup: 1034x
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
::: {.platypus-tip}
Building a **temporary data structure** just for faster lookups may feel redundant — but it is often the single most effective optimization.
:::
:::

## Detour: `std::set` vs `std::vector` — not like Python {background-color="#2d4f4f"}

In Python, `set` always beats `list` for lookups (1000x).
In C++, the picture is **very different**.

::: {.fragment}
100k lookups against a container of size N:

<table style="width:100%; table-layout:fixed;">
    <colgroup>
        <col style="width:11%;" />
        <col style="width:29.67%;" />
        <col style="width:29.67%;" />
        <col style="width:29.67%;" />
    </colgroup>
    <thead>
        <tr>
            <th style="white-space:nowrap;">N</th>
            <th>unsorted <code>vector</code> O(n)</th>
            <th><code>std::set</code> O(log n)</th>
            <th>sorted <code>vector</code> O(log n)</th>
        </tr>
    </thead>
    <tbody>
        <tr><td style="white-space:nowrap;">1K</td><td>1.5 ms</td><td>1.9 ms</td><td>1.7 ms</td></tr>
        <tr><td style="white-space:nowrap;">10K</td><td><strong>126 ms</strong></td><td>4.2 ms</td><td><strong>2.4 ms</strong></td></tr>
        <tr><td style="white-space:nowrap;">100K</td><td>1,258 ms</td><td>11.9 ms</td><td><strong>3.5 ms</strong></td></tr>
        <tr><td style="white-space:nowrap;">1M</td><td>12,690 ms</td><td>29.2 ms</td><td><strong>4.9 ms</strong></td></tr>
    </tbody>
</table>
:::

::: {.fragment}
At N=10K, `std::set` beats the linear scan by only **30x** — not 1000x like in Python.
And the **sorted vector beats `std::set` by up to 6x** — same O(log n), fewer cache misses.
:::

::: {.fragment}
::: {.platypus-tip}
In C++, **data layout often matters more than algorithmic complexity class**.
Prefer flat, contiguous containers unless insertions/deletions at scale make re-sorting too expensive.
:::
:::

## String concatenation in loops

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="48%"}
**Slow:**
```python
s = ""
for part in parts:
    s += part         # new string each time
```
:::

::: {.column width="4%"}
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
**Fast:**
```python
s = "".join(parts)    # one allocation
```
:::
::::

::: {.fragment}
```text
s += part:     2.63 ms
"".join():     0.14 ms     speedup: 18x
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
Strings are **immutable** in Python. Each `+=` copies the entire string so far.
That is $O(n^2)$ in total length.
:::

## Use builtins instead of manual loops

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="48%"}
**Slow:**
```python
total = 0
for x in xs:
    total += x
```
:::

::: {.column width="4%"}
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
**Fast:**
```python
total = sum(xs)
```
:::
::::

::: {.fragment}
```text
manual loop: 7.21 ms     sum(): 1.87 ms     speedup: 3.9x
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
Also applies to `min()`, `max()`, `any()`, `all()` — all run in C, not Python bytecode.
:::

::: {.fragment}
**The general principle:** push hot iterations into C-level code paths —
the same idea as pybind11, just within the standard library.
:::

## Memoization with `@lru_cache`

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="48%"}
**Slow:**
```python
def fib(n):
    if n < 2: return n
    return fib(n-1) + fib(n-2)
```
:::

::: {.column width="4%"}
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
**Fast:**
```python
from functools import lru_cache

@lru_cache(maxsize=None)
def fib(n):
    if n < 2: return n
    return fib(n-1) + fib(n-2)
```
:::
::::

::: {.fragment}
```text
fib(32) naive:   390 ms
fib(32) cached:  0.008 ms     speedup: 50,000x
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
Python's `@lru_cache` makes this a **one-line fix**.
Any **pure** function with **repeated inputs** is a candidate — DP subproblems, normalization, parsing.
:::

## Push numeric work to NumPy

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="48%"}
**Slow:**
```python
# Python nested loops
for i in range(n):
    for k in range(n):
        for j in range(n):
            C[i][j] += A[i][k] * B[k][j]
```
:::

::: {.column width="4%"}
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
**Fast:**
```python
import numpy as np

C = A @ B    # delegates to BLAS
```
:::
::::

::: {.fragment}
```text
Python loops (200x200): 436 ms
NumPy A @ B (200x200):  0.087 ms     speedup: 5000x
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
If Scalene shows high **Python time** on numeric loops,
NumPy (or moving to C++) is the answer — not micro-optimizing the loop.
:::

## Data containers: cost of abstraction

Creating 100k objects with three `float` fields:

| Container | Creation | Create + access | Relative |
|-----------|---------|-----------------|----------|
| `tuple` | 1.0 ms | 11 ms | **1x** |
| `class` / `dataclass` | 15 ms | 23 ms | **~2x** |
| `dataclass(slots=True)` | 14 ms | 22 ms | **~2x** |
| `NamedTuple` | 18 ms | 27 ms | **~2.4x** |
| Pydantic `BaseModel` | 51 ms | 59 ms | **~5x** |

::: {.fragment}
Pydantic validates and coerces **every field on every instantiation**.
That is invaluable at system boundaries — and ruinous in a hot loop.
:::

::: {.fragment}
::: {.platypus-tip}
Use Pydantic for **parsing external input** (API, config, files).
Use `dataclass(slots=True)` or plain tuples for **internal data** that gets created millions of times.
:::
:::
